title: Pediatric Urology Essentials

sections.title: Cryptorchidism
sections.content:
**Undescended Testis**

• 3% of term, 30% of preterm infants
• 80% descend by 3 months

**Evaluation:**
• Palpable vs non-palpable
• Retractile testis (normal variant)
• Ectopic testis

**Risks if Untreated:**
• Infertility
• Testicular cancer (even after surgery)
• Torsion

**Treatment:**
• Orchiopexy by 6-12 months
• Non-palpable: Laparoscopy to locate
• Orchiectomy if atrophic/intra-abdominal in older child

sections.title: Hypospadias
sections.content:
**Ventral urethral meatus**

• 1/250 male births
• Meatus location: Glanular, coronal, shaft, penoscrotal, perineal

**Associated Findings:**
• Chordee (ventral curvature)
• Hooded foreskin
• Meatal stenosis

**Contraindication:**
• DO NOT CIRCUMCISE (need foreskin for repair)

**Treatment:**
• Single-stage repair: 6-18 months
• Goals: Straight penis, meatus at tip, normal voiding

**DSD Workup if:**
• Severe hypospadias + cryptorchidism
• Karyotype, endocrine evaluation

sections.title: VUR
sections.content:
**Vesicoureteral Reflux**

**Grading I-V:**
• I-II: Ureter only or mild renal pelvis
• III: Moderate dilation
• IV-V: Severe dilation with tortuosity

**Presentation:**
• Febrile UTI workup
• Prenatal hydronephrosis
• Sibling screening

**Natural History:**
• Low grades often resolve
• Higher grades less likely

**Management:**
• Antibiotic prophylaxis
• Surveillance with VCUG
• Surgery if: Breakthrough UTIs, high grade, renal scarring

**Surgical Options:**
• Endoscopic injection (Deflux)
• Ureteral reimplantation

sections.title: UPJ Obstruction
sections.content:
**Ureteropelvic Junction Obstruction**

**Presentation:**
• Prenatal hydronephrosis (most common)
• Episodic flank pain (older children)
• UTI, hematuria after trauma

**Evaluation:**
• Renal ultrasound
• MAG3 diuretic renogram
• Half-time >20 min = obstruction

**Management:**
• Observation for mild cases
• Pyeloplasty for:
  - Decreasing function
  - Symptomatic
  - Worsening hydronephrosis

**Success Rate:** >95% for pyeloplasty

sections.title: Posterior Urethral Valves
sections.content:
**PUV - Most common cause of severe bilateral hydronephrosis in males**

**Prenatal:**
• Bilateral hydronephrosis
• Thick-walled bladder (keyhole sign)
• Oligohydramnios (severe)

**Postnatal:**
• Poor urinary stream
• Palpable bladder
• Elevated creatinine
• UTI/urosepsis

**Diagnosis:**
• VCUG: Dilated posterior urethra, trabeculated bladder

**Management:**
• Catheter drainage initially
• Valve ablation (endoscopic)
• Monitor renal function closely

**Long-term Concerns:**
• Valve bladder syndrome
• CKD (30-50%)
• VUR

keyTakeaways: Orchiopexy should be performed by 6-12 months
Never circumcise hypospadias - need foreskin for repair
Prenatal bilateral hydronephrosis in male = think PUV
PUV is leading cause of pediatric ESRD from urologic cause

reviewQuestions.question: A newborn male has bilateral hydronephrosis and a thick-walled bladder on prenatal ultrasound. After birth, he has a weak urinary stream. What is the most likely diagnosis?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Bilateral hydronephrosis with thick-walled bladder and weak stream in a male newborn is classic for posterior urethral valves. VCUG will show dilated posterior urethra and confirm the diagnosis.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected pediatric urology?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in pediatric urology. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of pediatric urology. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing pediatric urology, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
